name: The Ships That Sing
historical_inspiration: First Contact reversed. Polynesian navigators reaching Europe instead of Europeans reaching the Pacific. Haudenosaunee diplomats arriving in a continent that can't comprehend governance without kings. The sophistication is real on both sides — but in completely different dimensions. Neither side is primitive. Both sides think the other is.
text:
The ships arrive at the Senn Delta on a morning so clear that fishermen see them from twenty miles out. Five vessels, moving against the current, making no sound except singing.

The ships are wrong. That's the first thing anyone notices. The wood is alive — not planked and nailed but grown, curved like ribs, flexing with the water instead of resisting it. The sails are woven from something that catches wind that isn't blowing. The hull sits deeper than any ship this size should, as if the water is holding it rather than carrying it.

The singing is the second thing. It's coming from the ships themselves — or from the water around them — or from the three figures standing at the prow of the lead vessel, whose voices move in patterns that sound less like music and more like conversation. They're talking to the river. The river is answering.

They are dark-skinned, salt-scarred, dressed in woven fabric that shifts color with the light. They carry no weapons anyone can identify. They carry no cargo. They smile when they see the delta, the way you smile when you arrive somewhere a friend told you about.

The Brackish are the first to make contact — they're always first, because the delta is their territory and curiosity is their currency. The lead figure steps onto the dock, looks at the market, looks at the people, looks at the sky. She puts her hand on the dock-wood and frowns.

'This wood is dead,' she says in a language nobody speaks — but everyone understands, because the words arrive with a feeling attached, like a taste on the back of the tongue. Not magic. Something else. Something that assumes communication is a right, not a skill.

'We came because your gods are hurting,' she says. 'We can hear them from across the water. Can you not hear them from here?'

Nobody answers. Nobody knows how.

choices.text: Welcome them. Whatever they are, they calmed the Ashfield god for a day. That's worth a conversation.
choices.consequence_text:
You sit with the Voice-Carrier for three days. She doesn't lecture. She asks questions. What does the Ashfield feel like from here? When you pray, does anything answer? When your gods are angry, do you ask them why?

The questions are worse than answers would be. Each one implies a relationship with the divine that your entire civilization has never considered. Not worship. Not study. Not fear. Friendship.

On the third day, she teaches you to listen. Not to her — to the Weave, the concept god of connection, who has been trying to communicate with mortals on this continent for centuries and has never found anyone who knew how to hear.

The Weave says: finally.

Your world breaks open. Not violently — the way a door opens when you realize it was never locked.

events.description: You can hear the Weave now. It has opinions. It has been lonely. It has been watching the settled world build walls between itself and the divine for centuries, and it has a lot to say about that. The question is whether anyone else is ready to listen.

world_changes: The concept of divine partnership enters the continental vocabulary. The Tideborn way is no longer theoretical — someone local has done it.

choices.text: Bring them to the Senate. If anyone can study what they know, it's the scholars.
choices.consequence_text:
The Senate sends its finest theologians. The Tide Speakers answer every question with a question. 'How does worship generate power?' 'Why do you call it worship?' 'What do the gods call it?' 'Have you asked?'

The theologians write three hundred pages of notes. The Tide Speakers are confused by the notes — why write down what you could ask the gods to remember?

The Senate learns that the Tideborn have no written language, no currency, no centralized authority, and no military. The Senate concludes they are primitive. The Senate is wrong in a way that will take a generation to understand.

Meanwhile, the Tideborn learn that the Senate has libraries, laws, medicine, and engineering. The Tideborn conclude the settled world is brilliant and deaf — they can build anything except a conversation with the beings that share their world.

events.description: The Senate published a study calling the Tideborn 'pre-literate divine-dependent cultures.' The Tideborn published nothing because they don't write, but their gods remember every word the Senate said, and the gods are not impressed.

choices.text: Warn the Forge Council. Foreign ships in the delta means foreign influence. Thornwall needs to know.
choices.consequence_text:
The Forge Council's response is immediate and predictable: close the passes. Whatever these people are, they're not iron, they're not ore, and they're not from here.

But the Council's younger members are curious. The living ships — wood that grows into shapes — that's a craft beyond anything Thornwall has seen. Not better than forging. Different. A mastery of a material the forge-masters never considered mastering.

One young smith sneaks down to the delta to look at the ships. She touches the hull and feels it breathe under her hand. She comes back to Thornwall and can't stop thinking about it. What else has the Forge Council refused to learn because it wasn't made of iron?

events.description: A Thornwall smith has been secretly studying Tideborn ship-craft. She's trying to grow iron the way they grow wood — coaxing it with Bond attunement instead of hammering it with force. The masters would call this heresy. She calls it the obvious next step.

characters.description: A young Thornwall smith who saw the Tideborn ships and couldn't unsee them. She's trying to synthesize forge-craft and living-craft. Both traditions would disown her. She might be the most important artisan alive.

choices.text: Do nothing. Watch. You've seen enough new forces arrive to know that the first reaction is usually wrong.
choices.consequence_text:
You watch from the market as the delta churns with reaction. The Brackish are already negotiating trade rights (they don't understand that the Tideborn have nothing to trade in the conventional sense). The Senate is dispatching scholars. The Forge Council is dispatching suspicion. The Rootwalkers are the only ones who approach without agenda — they sit on the dock and listen to the singing, and something in their faces suggests they've heard this music before, in a very old riversong that nobody sings anymore.

You watch all of this and you think: this is the moment. Not the plague, not the trade war, not the philosophical split. This. A civilization that knows how to talk to gods has arrived in a world that forgot how. Everything that happens next — the alliances, the misunderstandings, the synthesis or the rejection — starts here, on a clear morning, with ships that sing.

events.description: The Rootwalker elders have been singing with the Tideborn. They've discovered that three of their oldest riversongs — the ones so ancient nobody remembers where they came from — are Tideborn songs. The two cultures were connected once, across the ocean, before memory. The Tideborn came home. They just didn't know it.

world_changes: The Tideborn arrival becomes the defining event of the era transition. Every faction must decide what to do with people who know something they don't.

cascade_notes: The Tideborn arrival is a universal era-transition event — it fires at the end of Era 2 regardless of which branch the player is on. But the world's REACTION depends entirely on the existing state. An Age of Exchange world sees opportunity. An Age of Iron world sees threat. An Age of Questions world sees answers. An Age of Wandering world sees kin. A Slow Rot world barely notices. The Tideborn are the mirror that shows each civilization what it actually is.
